title: Definitions
content: (a) 'acquisition' means directly or indirectly acquiring or agreeing to acquire shares, voting rights or assets of any enterprise, or control over management or control over assets; (b) 'agreement' includes any arrangement or understanding or action in concert whether or not formal, in writing, or intended to be enforceable; (c) 'cartel' includes an association of producers, sellers, distributors, traders or service providers who, by agreement amongst themselves, limit, control or attempt to control production, distribution, sale or price of, or trade in goods or provision of services; (d) 'Commission' means Competition Commission of India; (h) 'enterprise' means person or department of Government engaged in any activity relating to production, storage, supply, distribution, acquisition or control of articles or goods, or provision of services of any kind, or investment, or in business of acquiring, holding, underwriting or dealing with shares, debentures or other securities of any body corporate; (r) 'relevant market' means the market which may be determined by the Commission with reference to the relevant product market or the relevant geographic market or with reference to both the markets; (t) 'relevant product market' means a market comprising all those products or services which are regarded as interchangeable or substitutable by the consumer, by reason of characteristics of products or services, their prices and intended use.

title: Anti-Competitive Agreements
content: (1) No enterprise or association of enterprises or person or association of persons shall enter into any agreement in respect of production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or provision of services, which causes or is likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition within India. (2) Horizontal agreements (competitors) are presumed to have AAEC if they: (a) directly or indirectly determine purchase or sale prices; (b) limit or control production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or provision of services; (c) share market or source of production or provision of services by allocation of geographical area, type of goods/services, or number of customers; (d) directly or indirectly result in bid rigging or collusive bidding. (3) Vertical agreements (buyer-seller) are anti-competitive if they cause or likely to cause AAEC: (a) tie-in arrangement; (b) exclusive supply agreement; (c) exclusive distribution agreement; (d) refusal to deal; (e) resale price maintenance. (4) Burden of proof that agreement is not anti-competitive is on the enterprise. (5) Exemption for joint ventures increasing efficiency in production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or provision of services.

title: Abuse of Dominant Position
content: (1) No enterprise or group shall abuse its dominant position. (2) Abuse includes: (a) directly or indirectly imposing unfair or discriminatory conditions in purchase or sale of goods or services, or price in purchase or sale (including predatory price) of goods or services; (b) limiting or restricting production of goods or provision of services or market therefor, or technical or scientific development relating to goods or services to prejudice of consumers; (c) indulging in practices resulting in denial of market access in any manner; (d) making conclusion of contracts subject to acceptance by other parties of supplementary obligations which have no connection with subject of such contracts; (e) using dominant position in one relevant market to enter into or protect other relevant market. Explanation (a): 'dominant position' means position of strength, enjoyed by an enterprise, in the relevant market in India, which enables it to operate independently of competitive forces prevailing in the relevant market, or affect its competitors or consumers or the relevant market in its favour. Factors for determining dominance: market share, size and resources of enterprise, importance of competitors, economic power, vertical integration, dependence of consumers, entry barriers, social obligations, and any other factor.

title: Combination (Merger Control)
content: Acquisition of one or more enterprises by one or more persons, or merger or amalgamation of enterprises, is a combination if: (a) parties to combination jointly have assets in India > Rs. 2,000 crore OR turnover in India > Rs. 6,000 crore; (b) or group to which enterprise would belong has assets in India > Rs. 8,000 crore OR turnover in India > Rs. 24,000 crore; (c) or parties have assets worldwide > USD 1 billion (of which Rs. 1,000 crore in India) OR turnover worldwide > USD 3 billion (of which Rs. 3,000 crore in India); (d) or group has assets worldwide > USD 4 billion (of which Rs. 1,000 crore in India) OR turnover worldwide > USD 12 billion (of which Rs. 3,000 crore in India). Target exemption: No combination if target enterprise has assets in India < Rs. 350 crore AND turnover < Rs. 1,000 crore. De minimis exemption from notification. Green channel for combinations: Self-declaration of no overlap - deemed approved on filing.

title: Regulation of Combinations
content: (1) No person or enterprise shall enter into a combination which causes or is likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition within the relevant market in India and such a combination shall be void. (2) Parties to combination shall give notice to CCI within 30 days of: (a) approval of proposal relating to merger or amalgamation by board of directors; (b) execution of any agreement or other document for acquisition (prior to consummation). (2A) Combination involving acquisition of shares or voting rights shall not be consummated until CCI approval or expiry of 210 days from notice (amended from 30 days deemed approval). (3) Commission shall decide within 30 working days (extendable to 90/150 working days for Phase II review) whether combination has, or is likely to have, AAEC. Factors for assessment: actual and potential competition, entry barriers, buyer power, nature of products/services, market structure, likelihood of combination resulting in removal of vigorous and effective competitor, benefits to consumers.

title: Duties of Commission
content: Subject to provisions of this Act, it shall be the duty of the Commission to eliminate practices having adverse effect on competition, promote and sustain competition, protect interests of consumers and ensure freedom of trade carried on by other participants in markets in India. Commission shall, notwithstanding anything contained in any other law, have power to inquire into any alleged contravention of provisions of this Act. Commission may enter into MOU with any agency of foreign country for purpose of this Act. Commission's jurisdiction extends to conduct outside India if it has AAEC in India. Competition Appellate Tribunal (now NCLAT) hears appeals against CCI orders.

title: Inquiry into Anti-Competitive Agreements and Abuse of Dominance
content: (1) Commission may inquire into alleged contravention of Sections 3 or 4 either on its own motion or on receipt of information from any person, consumer or trade association, or reference from Central Government, State Government, or statutory authority. (3) Before forming prima facie opinion, Commission may call upon any person for such information as it may require. (4) If Commission is of prima facie opinion that there exists a case of contravention, it shall direct Director General to cause investigation. (5) On receipt of DG report, Commission may call upon parties concerned to show cause. (6) If Commission is of opinion that there is contravention, it may pass orders under Section 27 (for Section 3 contraventions) or Section 28 (for Section 4 contraventions). (7) Commission may close matter if it finds no contravention.

title: Orders by Commission after Inquiry into Agreements or Abuse
content: Where after inquiry Commission finds enterprise/person in contravention of Section 3 or Section 4, it may pass following orders: (a) Direct enterprise or association or person to discontinue and not to re-enter such agreement; (b) Direct enterprise to discontinue abuse of dominant position; (c) Impose penalty which may extend to 10% of average turnover for preceding three financial years (for cartel, penalty may extend to three times profit or 10% of turnover whichever is higher); (d) Direct modification of agreement; (e) Direct payment of costs; (f) Direct company and its directors/officers who are in default to pay penalty for their omissions or acts. Leniency: Lesser penalty available for cartel participants who make full, true and vital disclosure before DG investigation, provide evidence of cartel, cooperate throughout, cease contravention, and not be leader/instigator. First applicant gets up to 100% reduction.

title: Orders of Commission on Certain Combinations
content: Where Commission finds that combination has, or is likely to have, AAEC within relevant market in India: (a) Commission shall direct that combination shall not take effect; (b) Commission may propose modification to combination and if parties accept modification, approve the combination. If Commission finds combination will not have AAEC: Approve combination. Commission shall not approve a combination which creates monopoly or strengthens monopoly leading to AAEC. Waiting period: Parties cannot consummate combination until 210 days from notice or Commission decision, whichever is earlier. Penalty for gun-jumping (consummating without approval or during waiting period): Up to 1% of total turnover or assets, whichever is higher.

title: Acts Taking Place Outside India
content: Commission shall, notwithstanding that: (a) an agreement referred to in Section 3 has been entered into outside India; (b) any party to such agreement is outside India; (c) any enterprise abusing the dominant position is outside India; (d) a combination has taken place outside India; (e) any party to combination is outside India; (f) any other matter or practice or action arising out of such agreement or dominant position or combination is outside India - have power to inquire into such agreement or abuse of dominant position or combination if such agreement or dominant position or combination has, or is likely to have, an appreciable adverse effect on competition in India. This extraterritorial jurisdiction has been exercised in cases like Google (Android abuse), Hyundai/Kia (exclusive dealing with spare parts).

title: Penalty for Offences in Relation to Furnishing of Information
content: If any person, being a party to a combination: (a) fails to give notice under Section 6(2), he shall be liable to penalty which may extend to 1% of total turnover or assets, whichever is higher; (b) fails to give notice within the prescribed time limit, he shall be liable to penalty of Rs. 10,000 per day, maximum up to Rs. 1 crore; (c) makes any statement or furnishes any document which is false in material particulars, he shall be liable to penalty up to Rs. 1 crore; (d) omits to state any material fact, he shall be liable to penalty up to Rs. 1 crore. Section 42A: Penalty for failure to comply with CCI directions - up to Rs. 1 lakh per day, max Rs. 10 crore.

title: Lesser Penalty
content: (1) Commission may impose lesser penalty on producer, seller, distributor, trader or service provider included in any cartel, if such producer, seller, distributor, trader or service provider has made a full, true and vital disclosure in respect of alleged violations of Section 3 which is in the nature of cartel. (2) Lesser penalty under this section shall be granted by Commission only if: (a) application is made before issue of report by DG; (b) applicant has discontinued its participation in cartel; (c) applicant is not leader/instigator of cartel; (d) applicant has not coerced others to participate in cartel. CCI (Lesser Penalty) Regulations provide: First applicant - reduction up to 100%; Second - up to 50%; Third and subsequent - up to 30% (but not if DG has sufficient evidence).
